feat(memory): optimize relationship taxonomy (#121)

## Summary
- introduce a single relationship registry with tiered metadata for authorable, public, filterable, and default-expand relation sets
- restrict public association writes to the 11 semantic relationship types while keeping system-generated and legacy relationships readable/filterable via `DISCOVERED` compatibility
- align MCP/docs/graph behavior with the shared registry and sanitize benchmark comparison filenames so slash-containing branch names no longer break `bench-compare-branch`

## Validation
- `make test`
- `npm test` (in `mcp-sse-server`)
- `make bench-compare-branch BRANCH=main CONFIG=baseline BENCH=locomo-mini`
  - no significant change: `89.36% (210/235)` on this branch vs `89.36% (210/235)` on `main`
  - category deltas: `0.0%` across single-hop, temporal, multi-hop, open-domain, and complex reasoning

## Notes
- this change is intended to improve agent clarity and API/docs consistency rather than directly increase benchmark scores
- full feature-branch `locomo` eval also completed locally at `85.21% (1314/1542)` with judge off

commit.message:
feat(bench): add opt-in LoCoMo cat5 judge (#119)

## Summary
- add an opt-in LoCoMo category-5 judge using `BENCH_JUDGE_MODEL`,
`--judge`, and `--judge-model`
- preserve the default benchmark behavior with category 5 skipped unless
judge mode is enabled
- record corrected mini/full baselines and tone down benchmark claims to
reflect the split methodology

## Implementation
- add evidence-grounded cat-5 judging inside `LoCoMoEvaluator` with
operation-scoped LLM caching
- preserve deterministic scoring for the two category-5 rows that
already have canonical answers
- include judge metadata and per-question judge output in LoCoMo result
JSON
- add a 90s OpenAI request timeout so full judge-enabled runs fail fast
instead of hanging indefinitely
- add focused cat-5 judge unit tests and update benchmark/docs copy to
distinguish judge-off vs judge-on results

## Verification
- `make test`
  - `185 passed, 1 skipped, 12 deselected`
- `make bench-eval BENCH=locomo-mini CONFIG=baseline`
  - `89.27% (208/233)`
  - category 5: `N/A (71 skipped)`
- `BENCH_JUDGE_MODEL=gpt-4o make bench-eval BENCH=locomo-mini
CONFIG=baseline`
  - `89.80% (273/304)`
  - category 5: `91.55% (65/71)`
- `BENCH_JUDGE_MODEL=gpt-4o make bench-eval BENCH=locomo
CONFIG=baseline`
  - `87.56% (1739/1986)`
  - category 5: `95.74% (427/446)`
  - `0` judge skips/errors

## Notes
- this PR intentionally does not change AutoMem recall/ranking behavior;
remaining misses should be handled in follow-on branches for clean
benchmark comparison
- benchmark messaging now treats CORE `88.24%` as a published reference
point rather than a strict apples-to-apples leaderboard claim

commit.message:
fix(benchmarks): correct evaluator bugs, add agent guidelines, establish honest baseline (#117)

## Summary

- **AGENTS.md**: Added benchmarking section so all agents (Cursor,
Codex, Claude) know about the tiered bench system, key commands, and the
A/B workflow for recall changes.
- **Temporal evaluator fix**: `match_dates_fuzzy` was comparing question
dates vs memory dates, but temporal questions ("When did X happen?")
have no dates in the question—the date is in the expected answer. Also
added `session_datetime` decomposition into matchable word tokens for
Strategy 2. Temporal: 22.2% → 92.1%.
- **Category 5 skip**: Complex Reasoning was scoring 100% because the
dataset has no `answer` field (only `adversarial_answer`), so `"" in
<any string>` was always True. Now skipped and reported as N/A (requires
LLM judge per LoCoMo paper).
- **README updated**: Replaced inflated 90.53% with honest 89.27% (cats
1-4). Added note explaining evaluator bugs and pointing to
`EXPERIMENT_LOG.md`.
- **Category Breakdown table** added to `EXPERIMENT_LOG.md` so
per-category regressions are visible at a glance.

### Honest baseline (locomo-mini, Voyage 4, cats 1-4)

| Category | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall** | 76.97% (fake) | **89.27%** |
| Single-hop | 76.7% | 79.1% |
| Temporal | 22.2% (bug) | **92.1%** |
| Multi-hop | 46.2% | 46.2% |
| Open Domain | 96.5% | 96.5% |
| Complex | 100% (bug) | N/A |

## Test plan

- [x] `make bench-eval BENCH=locomo-mini` passes with correct scores
(89.27%)
- [x] Category 5 shows N/A (71 skipped)
- [x] No regressions in categories 1-4
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass (black, isort, flake8, conventional commit)

commit.message:
fix(benchmarks): handle possessive speaker names in LoCoMo (#116)

## What changed
- Fixed LoCoMoEvaluator._extract_speaker_from_question() to skip
possessive tokens before the capitalized-word match.
- Updated the possessive-name regex to support both ASCII (') and smart
(\u2019) apostrophes.
- Added unit coverage outside the benchmark suite for both Caroline's
and Caroline’s inputs.

## Why
The benchmark helper could return Carolines for possessive forms because
punctuation was stripped before the capitalized-word check. That made
the benchmark-side speaker-tag fallback inconsistent with the main
recall extractor.

## Testing
- AUTOMEM_API_TOKEN=test-token ADMIN_API_TOKEN=test-admin-token pytest
tests/test_locomo_speaker_extraction.py -q
- AUTOMEM_API_TOKEN=test-token ADMIN_API_TOKEN=test-admin-token pytest
tests/ -q --ignore=tests/benchmarks

commit.message:
fix: handle smart apostrophes in recall entity extraction (#115)

## What changed
- Fixed `_extract_query_entities` to recognize both ASCII (`'`) and
smart (`’`) apostrophes in possessive forms.
- Replaced a duplicated possessive check with explicit handling for
curly apostrophes.
- Updated possessive-name regex to support both apostrophe styles.
- Added regression tests for:
  - `Caroline's` (ASCII apostrophe)
  - `Caroline’s` (smart apostrophe)

## Why
- Many clients send smart quotes, so possessive names like `Caroline’s`
should be parsed the same as `Caroline's`.
- Before this change, curly-apostrophe possessives could be missed by
entity extraction, reducing recall quality for auto-decomposed queries.

## Testing
- ✅ `source .venv/bin/activate && pytest -q`
  - Result: 156 passed, 11 skipped

Co-authored-by: Jack Arturo <info@verygoodplugins.com>

commit.message:
feat(config): add QDRANT_HOST + QDRANT_PORT as alternative to QDRANT_URL (#112)

## What

Adds `QDRANT_HOST` and `QDRANT_PORT` (default `6333`) as an alternative
way to configure Qdrant, so you can point AutoMem at an internal
hostname without constructing a full URL.

**Priority:** `QDRANT_URL` always takes precedence. If only
`QDRANT_HOST` is set, the URL is constructed as
`http://<QDRANT_HOST>:<QDRANT_PORT>`.

## Why

On Railway (and similar platforms), Qdrant is reachable via an internal
hostname like `qdrant.railway.internal`. Previously you had to set
`QDRANT_URL=http://qdrant.railway.internal:6333`, which requires knowing
the port. With `QDRANT_HOST=qdrant.railway.internal` alone (using the
default port) this becomes a single, obvious env var.

This also avoids the ~80ms HTTP latency that comes from routing through
Railway's public proxy — internal networking is direct.

## Changes

- **`automem/config.py`** (already done, production-proven): reads
`QDRANT_HOST` and constructs the URL if `QDRANT_URL` is not set
- **`Dockerfile`**: documents `QDRANT_URL`, `QDRANT_API_KEY`,
`QDRANT_HOST`, and `QDRANT_PORT` as ENV declarations with an explanatory
comment
- **`.env.example`**: adds `QDRANT_HOST=` and `QDRANT_PORT=6333` with a
comment explaining it as an alternative to `QDRANT_URL`

## Backwards compatibility

Fully backwards compatible. Existing deployments using `QDRANT_URL` are
unaffected — it still takes precedence.

## Notes

Railway internal networking for Qdrant can be flaky in some
configurations (HTTP is the current workaround for gRPC issues). This
change is an improvement but may need environment-specific testing —
particularly on Railway with private networking enabled.

Railway internal networking for Qdrant can be flaky in some
configurations (HTTP is the current workaround for gRPC issues). This
change is an improvement but may need environment-specific testing —
particularly on Railway with private networking enabled.

commit.message:
fix(qdrant): prevent silent vector dimension mismatch, set Voyage as recommended default (#108)

## Summary

Fixes #107 (silent vector dimension mismatch on update) and closes #84
(change default embedding model).

**The bug:** Changing docker-compose `VECTOR_SIZE` default from
3072→1024 silently broke existing deployments. The dimension mismatch
raised `ValueError` which `init_qdrant` caught generically, disabling
Qdrant without a clear error. Server started but vector search was dead.

**The fix:**
- `VectorDimensionMismatchError` (RuntimeError, not ValueError)
propagates as **fatal startup error** with actionable instructions
- `VECTOR_SIZE_AUTODETECT=true` by default — existing collections are
automatically adopted on startup
- OpenAI model auto-upgrades from `text-embedding-3-small` to
`text-embedding-3-large` when effective dimension > 1536d
- `/health` endpoint now exposes `vector_dimensions` (configured,
effective, collection, mismatch flag)
- All defaults synced to 1024d / Voyage / text-embedding-3-small across
config, docker-compose, .env.example, and 6 doc files

### Every upgrade scenario covered:

| User scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| **New install** | Gets 1024d Voyage default |
| **Existing 3072d collection** | Autodetect adopts 3072d, OpenAI
auto-upgrades to `text-embedding-3-large` |
| **Existing 768d collection** | Autodetect adopts 768d, OpenAI small
works fine at 768d |
| **Existing 1024d collection** | Dimensions match, no change |
| **Explicit `EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=openai` + `VECTOR_SIZE=3072`** |
Auto-upgrades model to large, works |
| **Explicit `EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=openai` + `VECTOR_SIZE=768`** | Uses
small model, works |
| **`VECTOR_SIZE_AUTODETECT=false`** | Fatal error with clear fix
instructions |

### Files changed (14):
- `automem/utils/validation.py` — `VectorDimensionMismatchError`,
autodetect default=true
- `automem/stores/runtime_clients.py` — Let mismatch error propagate
(not caught by ValueError handler)
- `automem/embedding/provider_init.py` — `_resolve_openai_model()`
auto-upgrade safety
- `automem/api/health.py` — `vector_dimensions` in health response
- `automem/config.py` — VECTOR_SIZE=1024,
EMBEDDING_MODEL=text-embedding-3-small
- `docker-compose.yml` — Consistent VECTOR_SIZE default
- `.env.example` — Updated with comments
- `tests/test_vector_size_safety.py` — 19 new tests
- `tests/test_embedding_providers.py` — Fix 2 flaky tests (env leak)
- Docs: CLAUDE.md, INSTALLATION.md, ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLES.md,
QDRANT_SETUP.md, MIGRATIONS.md

## Test plan

- [x] 19 new tests covering autodetect, strict mode, error propagation,
model auto-upgrade
- [x] Fixed 2 pre-existing flaky tests (host env leaking
`VOYAGE_API_KEY`/`EMBEDDING_PROVIDER`)
- [x] Full suite: 173 passed, 0 failed, 11 skipped (integration)
- [ ] Integration test with existing 3072d Qdrant collection (manual)
- [ ] Integration test with fresh install (manual)

commit.message:
fix(consolidation): reduce decay rate, add importance floor, filter archived memories (#78) (#105)

## Summary

Fixes the overly aggressive memory decay formula that destroyed 84% of
production memories within 30 days, regardless of importance.

**The problem:** `base_decay_rate=0.1` (exponential) meant a 30-day
memory had `exp(-3) = 0.05` relevance, and a 90-day memory had `exp(-9)
= 0.0001`. Production audit showed:
- 84.3% of 9,318 memories classified as "archive" (relevance < 0.05)
- 2,736 high-importance (≥0.7) memories with relevance < 0.1 ("unfairly
killed")
- 409 protected types (Decision/Insight) with near-zero relevance
- Average relevance score: 0.049

**The fix:**
- **Decay rate:** `0.1 → 0.01` (configurable via
`CONSOLIDATION_BASE_DECAY_RATE`), changing the half-life from ~7 to ~69
days
- **Importance floor:** `relevance = max(calculated, importance * 0.3)`
— a memory with importance 0.8 can never decay below 0.24. Configurable
via `CONSOLIDATION_IMPORTANCE_FLOOR_FACTOR`
- **Archive filter:** Added `coalesce(m.archived, false) = false` to
keyword search (was only filtered in trending). Added post-filter for
Qdrant vector search. Consolidated archived flag syncs to Qdrant
payload.
- **Rehabilitation scripts:** `scripts/audit_relevance.py` (read-only
damage assessment) and `scripts/rescore_relevance.py` (one-time
recalculation with `--dry-run` and `--target railway` options)

**Deployment safety:** Delete/archive thresholds remain at `0.0`
(disabled). After merge and deploy:
1. Run `rescore_relevance.py --target railway --dry-run` to preview
2. Run `rescore_relevance.py --target railway` to rehabilitate (avg
score: 0.049 → 0.376)
3. Monitor for 1 week, then enable conservative thresholds

## Benchmark Results

| Benchmark | Baseline | This PR | Delta |
|-----------|----------|---------|-------|
| locomo-mini (304 Qs) | 76.97% | 76.97% | +0.0% |
| locomo-full (1986 Qs) | 80.06% | 79.51% | -0.55% (within variance) |

No regression — expected since benchmark snapshots use fresh data
unaffected by production decay.

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests pass (`make test` — 110 passed, 11 skipped)
- [x] Consolidation engine tests updated for new formula
- [x] locomo-mini: 76.97% (no change)
- [x] locomo-full: 79.51% (-0.55%, within variance)
- [x] Rescore dry-run against production: avg 0.049 → 0.376, 9,257
improved
- [x] Production backup taken before any changes
- [ ] Post-merge: run rescore on production
- [ ] Post-rescore: monitor decay health for 1 week
- [ ] After 1 week: enable thresholds
(CONSOLIDATION_DELETE_THRESHOLD=0.01,
CONSOLIDATION_ARCHIVE_THRESHOLD=0.05)

Closes #78

fix(mcp-sse): sync tool schemas for SSE/MCP parity (#104)

## Summary

Fixes the SSE server side of #90 — tool description drift between SSE
and MCP transports.

- **`associate_memories.type`**: Was unconstrained string, now enum with
all 16 valid relationship types (synced from `automem/config.py
RELATIONSHIP_TYPES`)
- **`update_memory`**: Added missing `type` and `confidence` properties
(MCP package already had these)
- **All tools**: Added parameter-level `description` fields to every
property
- **All tools**: Added MCP `annotations` (`readOnlyHint`,
`destructiveHint`) for spec compliance
- **Shared constants**: Extracted `RELATION_TYPES` and `MEMORY_TYPES`
arrays so they're easy to keep in sync

### What's NOT in this PR (separate repo / follow-up)

The MCP npm package (`verygoodplugins/mcp-automem`) still needs:
- `per_query_limit`, `sort`, `format` added to `recall_memory`
- `store_memory` expanded from 6 to 13 properties
- `outputSchema` definitions (both SSE and MCP)

## Test plan

- [x] All 7 existing `mcp-sse-server` tests pass
- [x] No application code changes — only tool schema metadata
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass (conventional commit, secrets check,
trailing whitespace)

Closes the SSE-server portion of #90.

commit.message:
feat(recall): add min_score threshold and adaptive floor filtering (#73) (#101)

## Summary

- Adds `min_score` query parameter to `/recall` for explicit noise
filtering
- Adds `RECALL_MIN_SCORE` env var (default `0.0`) as server-wide default
- Adds `RECALL_ADAPTIVE_FLOOR` (default `true`) which detects steep
score dropoffs and prunes low-quality result tails automatically
- Includes `score_filter` metadata in response when filtering is active

## Benchmark Results

| Metric | Baseline | This PR | Delta |

commit.message:
feat(bench): benchmark testing infrastructure for rapid iteration (#97)

## Summary

Overhauls benchmark testing infrastructure to enable rapid A/B iteration
on recall quality. Previously, every benchmark run took 30-120 minutes
and had a ~100% failure rate due to fragile shell scripts, infinite
cleanup loops, and silent error swallowing.

**Key changes:**
- **Reliability fixes**: Replace `set -e` + `sleep 10` with `set -uo
pipefail` + proper health-check retry loops in shell scripts. Fix
infinite `cleanup_test_data()` loop with `max_iterations` guard. Replace
`except: pass` with logged warnings.
- **Mini benchmark mode**: `--conversations 0,1` flag runs 2 of 10
LoCoMo conversations (~1,200 memories, ~400 questions) in <5 minutes
instead of 30+
- **Snapshot infrastructure**: Separate ingestion from evaluation via
`--ingest-only` / `--eval-only` flags + Docker volume snapshot/restore
scripts. Ingest once, evaluate many configs.
- **Batch memory API**: `POST /memory/batch` endpoint reduces 5,882
individual POSTs to ~60 batch requests (single embedding call, single
UNWIND Cypher, single Qdrant upsert)
- **Comparison pipeline**: `compare_results.py` for side-by-side
accuracy tables, `compare_branch.sh` and `compare_configs.sh` for
automated A/B workflows
- **Lab config fix**: `restart_api_with_config()` now writes
`.env.bench` and uses `--env-file` instead of subprocess env (which
Docker ignored)

## New Makefile targets

```
make bench-mini-locomo       # 2 conversations, <5 min
make bench-mini              # Both benchmarks, mini mode
make bench-ingest BENCH=locomo        # Ingest + snapshot
make bench-eval BENCH=locomo CONFIG=baseline  # Restore + eval (~2-3 min)
make bench-compare BENCH=locomo CONFIG=bm25   # A/B config comparison
make bench-compare-branch BRANCH=feat/x       # Branch comparison
```

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests pass (1 pre-existing skip due to Voyage env key)
- [x] Route contract updated for new `POST /memory/batch`
- [x] Run `make bench-mini-locomo` end-to-end
- [ ] Run `make bench-ingest` + `make bench-eval` snapshot cycle
- [ ] Test `compare_results.py` with two result files

commit.message:
fix: FalkorDB data not persisting across restarts (#99)

## Problem

FalkorDB's entrypoint script (`run.sh`) always appends `--dir
$FALKORDB_DATA_PATH` **after** `$REDIS_ARGS`, so the `--dir /data` in
`REDIS_ARGS` is silently overridden by the default
`FALKORDB_DATA_PATH=/var/lib/falkordb/data`.

This means Redis writes its RDB dumps and AOF files to
`/var/lib/falkordb/data` (ephemeral container filesystem) instead of
`/data` (the Docker volume). On any container restart or recreate, all
FalkorDB graph data is lost.

The failure is silent — FalkorDB comes back healthy with
`memory_count=0` while Qdrant still has all vectors, making it look like
a FalkorDB bug rather than a persistence misconfiguration.

## Fix

- Remove `--dir /data` from `REDIS_ARGS` (it was being ignored anyway)
- Set `FALKORDB_DATA_PATH=/data` so the entrypoint's own `--dir` flag
points to the volume mount

## Verification

After this change:
- `redis-cli CONFIG GET dir` returns `/data` (the volume) instead of
`/var/lib/falkordb/data`
- `BGSAVE` writes to the volume-mounted path
- Data survives container restarts

commit.message:
fix: FalkorDB data not persisting across restarts

FalkorDB's entrypoint (run.sh) always appends --dir $FALKORDB_DATA_PATH
after $REDIS_ARGS, so the --dir /data in REDIS_ARGS was silently
overridden. Redis was writing to /var/lib/falkordb/data (ephemeral
container storage) instead of /data (the Docker volume).

This caused silent data loss on every container restart — FalkorDB would
come back with memory_count=0 while Qdrant (which persists correctly)
still had all vectors, requiring a manual recovery step each time.

Fix: Remove --dir from REDIS_ARGS and set FALKORDB_DATA_PATH=/data so
the entrypoint uses the volume-mounted path.

feat(viewer): consolidate stable core and split-ready compatibility (#94)

## Summary
- Consolidates visualizer work into `feat/visualizer-stable-core`
(stable core only)
- Switches AutoMem `/viewer` from in-process static SPA serving to
standalone compatibility redirect/bootstrap using `GRAPH_VIEWER_URL`
- Preserves `/viewer` and `/viewer/*` compatibility routes while keeping
hash token handling client-side
- Adds `VIEWER_ALLOWED_ORIGINS` CORS allowlist support and preflight
handling
- Makes hand controls opt-in in graph viewer via
`VITE_ENABLE_HAND_CONTROLS=false` default
- Adds standalone viewer runtime files and split-repo CI under
`packages/graph-viewer/.github/workflows/ci.yml`
- Removes committed `.vite` artifacts and stale visualizer
simplification doc
- Drops frontend build stage from AutoMem root Dockerfile

## Config Changes
AutoMem env vars:
- `ENABLE_GRAPH_VIEWER=true`
- `GRAPH_VIEWER_URL=https://<viewer-domain>`
- `VIEWER_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=https://<viewer-domain>[,https://... ]`

Viewer env var:
- `VITE_ENABLE_HAND_CONTROLS=false` (opt-in)

## Verification
- `make lint` ✅
- `AUTOMEM_API_TOKEN=test-token ADMIN_API_TOKEN=test-admin-token
EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=auto make test` ✅ (`152 passed, 1 skipped, 10
deselected`)
- `cd packages/graph-viewer && npm run build` ✅

### Curl checks (local API on :8012)
- `curl -i http://localhost:8012/graph/snapshot?limit=10 -H 'X-API-Key:
test-token'` → `200 OK`
- `curl -i http://localhost:8012/viewer/?foo=bar` → `200 OK` (bootstrap
HTML)
- `curl -i http://localhost:8012/viewer/assets/index.js?v=1` → `302
FOUND` to `https://viewer.example.com/assets/index.js?v=1`
- `curl -i -X OPTIONS http://localhost:8012/graph/snapshot -H 'Origin:
https://viewer.example.com' -H 'Access-Control-Request-Method: GET' -H
'Access-Control-Request-Headers: X-API-Key, Content-Type'` → `200 OK`,
`Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://viewer.example.com`

## Notes
- Python `make build` target does not exist in this repo; verification
used lint + tests per current Makefile.
- Split branch for standalone repo has been created with history
preservation via `git subtree split --prefix=packages/graph-viewer -b
split/automem-graph-viewer`.

refactor(app): decompose app.py into runtime modules (phase 1) (#92)

## Summary
- decomposes `app.py` into focused runtime modules while preserving API
behavior
- extracts auth, service init/access, embedding, enrichment,
consolidation, sync, recall helpers, runtime wiring, and environment
setup into `automem/*` modules
- removes dead legacy route scaffolding and keeps `app.py` as
composition/bootstrap
- keeps monkeypatch-heavy test compatibility via runtime getter/binding
patterns

## Size/Structure Impact
- `app.py` reduced to ~476 lines
- added modular runtime files under:
  - `automem/api/*`
  - `automem/analytics/*`
  - `automem/classification/*`
  - `automem/consolidation/*`
  - `automem/embedding/*`
  - `automem/enrichment/*`
  - `automem/search/*`
  - `automem/stores/*`
  - `automem/sync/*`
  - `automem/runtime_*.py`
  - `automem/service_*.py`

## Verification
- `make test`
  - `148 passed, 1 skipped, 10 deselected`
- `AUTOMEM_RUN_INTEGRATION_TESTS=1 make test-integration`
  - `10 passed, 149 deselected`

## Notes
- no endpoint behavior/schema changes intended in this PR
- this is the decomposition foundation for future focused refactors

commit.message:
fix: code quality improvements from CodeRabbit review (#85)

## Summary

Addresses code quality issues identified during CodeRabbit review of
v0.11.0 changes. All fixes are backwards-compatible with no behavioral
changes to production code paths.

### API & Validation
- Add UUID validation on `GET /memory/<id>` — returns 400 for invalid
IDs instead of querying with bad data
- Use `ResponseReturnValue` return type on memory API endpoint
- Wrap `on_access` callback in try/except so failed access tracking
doesn't 500 successful reads

### Embedding Providers
- Respect `OPENAI_BASE_URL` environment variable when deciding whether
to send `dimensions` param and naming the provider
- Add return type hints to `VoyageEmbeddingProvider` special methods
(`__init__`, `__enter__`, `__exit__`, `__del__`)

### Type Safety & Correctness
- Modernize type annotations in `utils/time.py` (use `str | None` union
syntax, lowercase `tuple`)
- Narrow `_normalize_timestamp` signature to `str` (matching actual
implementation)
- Handle naive timestamps as UTC in `_normalize_timestamp`

### Test Quality
- Replace all non-UUID test memory IDs with valid UUIDs to match the
UUID-only contract
- Flatten association test payload to match actual API shape (top-level
fields, not nested `properties`)
- Add type hints to `MockQdrantClient.search` and test helper
`Graph.query`
- Add `test_get_memory_invalid_id` test for the new UUID validation
- Use `@pytest.mark.usefixtures` for tests that don't reference the
fixture directly

### Documentation & Tooling
- Update CLAUDE.md provider priority to match actual code (Voyage-first)
- Add VoyageEmbeddingProvider to implementation list
- Add pytest and CodeRabbit CLI to pre-commit pre-push hooks

## Test plan

- [x] All 137 unit tests pass
- [x] All pre-commit hooks pass (black, isort, flake8, trailing
whitespace, detect-secrets)
- [x] All pre-push hooks pass (pytest, coderabbit local review)
- [x] No changes to production behavior — only validation, error
handling, and type safety improvements

commit.message:
feat: just-in-time enrichment on recall (#86) (#88)

## Summary

- When a memory is recalled before the async enrichment worker processes it, run **lightweight inline enrichment** (entity extraction + summary, ~50ms) so entity tags and summaries are immediately available
- Sets `enriched=true` but **not** `processed=true` — the async worker still runs the expensive operations (temporal links, pattern detection, semantic neighbors) later
- Guarded by `JIT_ENRICHMENT_ENABLED` env var (default: `true`)

Closes #86

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `app.py` | New `jit_enrich_lightweight()` function — entity extraction, summary, tag generation inline |
| `automem/api/recall.py` | JIT pass after result assembly, `jit_enriched_count` in response metadata |
| `automem/config.py` | `JIT_ENRICHMENT_ENABLED` env var |
| `tests/test_app.py` | 5 new tests (basic JIT, empty content, no graph, recall integration, skip logic) |
| `CLAUDE.md` | Document new env var |
| `docs/ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLES.md` | Document new env var + explain JIT behavior |

## Test plan

- [x] `make test` — 141 passed, 0 failed
- [ ] Manual: `POST /memory` → immediate `GET /recall` → verify `summary`, entity tags, `jit_enriched` in response
- [ ] Verify async worker still processes the memory later (`processed` transitions to `true`)
- [ ] `make test-integration` with Docker

feat: add LongMemEval benchmark harness (ICLR 2025) (#83)

## Summary

- Cherry-picks LongMemEval benchmark code from
`claude/longmemeval-benchmark-5vzWE` branch
- Fixes recall response format mismatch — AutoMem nests memory data
under `result["memory"]`, benchmark code now flattens this correctly
- 20-question smoke test on `single-session-user` type scores **85.0%
(17/20)**, competitive with top systems

## Benchmark Results

| System | Accuracy |
|--------|----------|
| Mastra OM (gpt-5-mini) | 94.9% |
| Emergence Internal | 86.0% |
| **AutoMem (baseline)** | **85.0%** |
| Mastra OM (gpt-4o) | 84.2% |
| Oracle gpt-4o | 82.4% |
| Supermemory | 81.6% |

3 "wrong" answers are semantically correct but fail strict string
matching (e.g. "University of Melbourne" vs "University of Melbourne in
Australia"). Using `--llm-eval` would likely push to ~100%.

## Files

- `tests/benchmarks/longmemeval/` — benchmark harness (configs,
evaluator, test runner)
- `test-longmemeval-benchmark.sh` — convenience shell script
- Makefile/CLAUDE.md/.gitignore updates for benchmark integration

## Running

```bash
# Requires MEMORY_CONTENT_HARD_LIMIT=50000 on the server (sessions avg 10k chars)
AUTOMEM_TEST_BASE_URL=http://localhost:8001 \
OPENAI_API_KEY=$OPENAI_API_KEY \
python tests/benchmarks/longmemeval/test_longmemeval.py \
  --config baseline --max-questions 20
```

## Test plan

- [x] `make test` passes (19/19)
- [x] 20-question smoke test completes end-to-end
- [x] Per-question memory cleanup works (no leftover data)
- [ ] Full 500-question run (future work)

commit.message:
feat(lab): Recall Quality Lab + fix relevance_score Qdrant sync (#81)

## Summary

- Adds a **Recall Quality Lab** toolkit (`scripts/lab/`) for data-driven
recall optimization using production data snapshots
- Fixes a critical bug where `relevance_score` was **never synced to
Qdrant**, making it invisible to ~90% of recall results (vector search
path)
- Adds `SEARCH_WEIGHT_RELEVANCE` config (default 0.0) so relevance can
be enabled without code changes

## What's in the lab

| Script | Purpose |
|--------|---------|
| `scripts/lab/clone_production.sh` | One-command Railway → local Docker
snapshot pipeline |
| `scripts/lab/create_test_queries.py` | Generate test queries from prod
data via GPT-4o-mini |
| `scripts/lab/run_recall_test.py` | Test harness with Recall@K, MRR,
NDCG metrics + paired t-test comparison |
| `scripts/lab/configs/*.json` | Named scoring configs for A/B testing |

## Bug fix: relevance_score Qdrant sync

**Root cause**: `_store_embedding_in_qdrant()` never included
`relevance_score` in the Qdrant payload. Consolidation decay updated it
in FalkorDB only. Since vector search returns Qdrant payloads as the
`memory` dict, the scoring function's `memory.get("relevance_score")`
always returned `None` for vector results.

**Fix**:
- Add `relevance_score` to Qdrant upsert payload (`app.py`)
- Sync `relevance_score` to Qdrant during consolidation decay and
forgetting (`consolidation.py`, 4 locations)

## Lab test results (100 real-world queries, 8637 memories)

```
Baseline: R@5=0.920  R@10=0.940  MRR=0.800  NDCG@10=0.834

Sweep SEARCH_WEIGHT_RELEVANCE:
   Value     R@5    R@10     MRR    NDCG
     0.0   0.920   0.940   0.800   0.834
    0.15   0.920   0.940   0.800   0.834
    0.30   0.920   0.940   0.800   0.834
    0.50   0.920   0.940   0.800   0.834
```

Zero regression at any weight — safe to deploy. Identical scores confirm
issue #78: decay crushes all relevance scores to near-zero (~0.069 avg),
so the signal is too uniform to affect ranking.

## Relates to

- #78 — Decay running but not affecting recall (this PR fixes the
plumbing; decay curve redesign is next)

## Test plan

- [x] 136 unit tests pass
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass (black, flake8, isort)
- [x] CodeRabbit review findings addressed
- [x] Lab sweep confirms zero regression across all relevance weights
- [ ] LoCoMo benchmark (run after merge)

commit.message: docs: Update environment variables

fix(lab): address CodeRabbit review — type hints, f-strings, validation

- Add return type annotations to all functions
- Remove placeholder-free f-strings (F541)
- Remove unused env_args variable (F841)
- Validate --restore-only requires explicit snapshot name

fix(recall): sync relevance_score to Qdrant payload for vector search

Root cause: relevance_score was only stored in FalkorDB graph nodes but
never included in Qdrant point payloads. Since ~90% of recall results
come from vector search (Qdrant), the scoring component was invisible.

Changes:
- Add relevance_score to Qdrant upsert payload (app.py)
- Sync relevance_score to Qdrant during consolidation decay (consolidation.py)
- Sync relevance_score to Qdrant during forgetting phase (consolidation.py)
- Fix clone_production.sh to use FALKORDB_TCP_PROXY env var
- Fix create_test_queries.py prompt for 0-based memory indexing

Lab testing with 100 real-world queries shows zero regression at any
relevance weight (0.0-0.50), confirming the change is safe to deploy.
The lack of differentiation confirms issue #78: decay crushes all
relevance scores to near-zero (~0.069 avg), making the signal uniform.

fix(lab): address CodeRabbit review findings

- Handle empty dirname when output path has no directory component
- Require --config when using --compare flag (was silently using None)

feat(lab): add Recall Quality Lab for data-driven recall optimization

Adds a testing framework to clone production data, generate real-world
test queries, and compare recall algorithm variants with statistical rigor.

- scripts/lab/clone_production.sh: one-command Railway → local Docker snapshot
- scripts/lab/create_test_queries.py: stratified sampling + GPT-4o-mini question gen
- scripts/lab/run_recall_test.py: IR metrics (Recall@K, MRR, NDCG) with paired t-test
- 4 test configs for issue #78 hypotheses (baseline, relevance weight, archive filter, combined)
- SEARCH_WEIGHT_RELEVANCE env var wires consolidation relevance_score into recall scoring (default 0.0, no behavior change)
- Makefile targets: lab-clone, lab-queries, lab-test, lab-compare, lab-sweep

Closes #75 (partial — provides the regression test infrastructure)
Related: #78 (decay not affecting recall)

feat(embedding): add Voyage AI embedding provider (#67)

## Summary
Add Voyage AI as an embedding provider option, supporting the Voyage 4
family (voyage-4, voyage-4-large, voyage-4-lite) with configurable
dimensions.

## Changes
- New `VoyageEmbeddingProvider` class with batch support (128/batch),
retry logic, and rate limit handling
- Added `voyage` as explicit `EMBEDDING_PROVIDER` option
- Voyage is first in auto-selection priority when `VOYAGE_API_KEY` is
set
- Configurable via `VOYAGE_API_KEY`, `VOYAGE_MODEL` env vars

## Why Voyage?
- High quality embeddings (approaches voyage-3-large quality)
- Generous free tier
- Shared embedding space across the voyage-4 family (can upgrade models
without re-embedding)
- Complements the existing OpenAI and Ollama providers

## Summary
Add a GET endpoint to retrieve a single memory by ID, completing the
basic CRUD operations.

missing individual read by ID. This is useful for inspecting specific
memories and building UIs.

fix(utils): handle numeric timestamps and non-string values in time parser (#69)

## Summary
Make `_parse_iso_datetime` more robust by handling numeric unix
timestamps and non-string input types.

## Changes
- Accept `int`/`float` unix timestamps (converts via
`datetime.fromtimestamp`)
- Gracefully handle non-string, non-numeric input (returns `None`
instead of raising)
- Existing ISO string parsing behavior unchanged

## Motivation
Prevents `TypeError`/`AttributeError` when metadata contains numeric
timestamps instead of ISO strings, which can happen with data from
external sources or migrations.

feat(embedding): support OpenAI-compatible providers via OPENAI_BASE_URL (#76)

## Summary

Adds configurable base URL support to the OpenAI embedding provider so
users can point at **any OpenAI-compatible API** (OpenRouter, LiteLLM,
vLLM, Azure, etc.) with just env vars — no code changes needed.

- Added `base_url` param to `OpenAIEmbeddingProvider`, wired through
from `OPENAI_BASE_URL` env var
- The `dimensions` request parameter is **only sent to OpenAI's own
API** (`api.openai.com`) — most compatible providers don't support it,
so it's omitted for custom base URLs
- Provider name reflects the endpoint: `openai:model` vs
`openai-compatible:model`
- Documented in ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLES.md, README, CLAUDE.md,
INSTALLATION.md, and .env.example

### Usage

```bash
EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=openai
OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-your-key
OPENAI_BASE_URL=https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
EMBEDDING_MODEL=openai/text-embedding-3-small
VECTOR_SIZE=768
```

## Test plan

- [x] All 120 existing unit tests pass
- [x] New tests: `test_openai_provider_initialization_with_base_url` —
verifies `base_url` passthrough to SDK
- [x] New tests:
`test_openai_provider_omits_dimensions_for_compatible_provider` —
verifies `dimensions` kwarg is omitted for non-OpenAI URLs
- [x] New tests: `test_is_openai_native_detection` — verifies URL
detection logic
- [x] Pre-commit hooks pass (black, isort, flake8, conventional commit)
- [ ] Manual test with OpenRouter (or other compatible provider) if
someone has a key

Closes #70

fix: validate embedding items and fix line length

fix: guard against bool values in numeric timestamp parsing

- Add check to exclude bool values before numeric isinstance check
- Prevents True/False from being treated as unix timestamps (1/0)
- Bool is a subclass of int in Python, so needs explicit exclusion

Addresses CodeRabbit review comment.

fix: add missing metadata parsing and access tracking to GET /memory/<id>

- Call parse_metadata_field on serialized node's metadata for consistency with by_tag endpoint
- Call on_access callback to update last_accessed timestamp for consistency with by_tag endpoint

Addresses CodeRabbit review comments.

fix: add defensive validation and improve error handling in Voyage provider

- Add defensive validation for API response structure before accessing data['data']
- Check for 'data' key existence and validate it's a list, raise ValueError instead of KeyError
- Retry 5xx server errors in addition to 429 rate limit errors
- Move import time to module level for better organization

Addresses CodeRabbit review comments.

fix(utils): handle numeric timestamps and non-string values in time parser

Make _parse_iso_datetime more robust by:
- Accepting int/float unix timestamps (converts via datetime.fromtimestamp)
- Gracefully handling non-string, non-numeric input (returns None)
- Keeping existing ISO string parsing behavior unchanged

This prevents TypeErrors when metadata contains numeric timestamps
instead of ISO strings.

feat(api): add GET /memory/<id> endpoint

Add a GET endpoint to retrieve a single memory by ID. This completes
the basic CRUD operations (the existing API had create, update, delete,
and list/search but was missing individual read).

Returns the full serialized memory node with status wrapper.

ci: use PAT for release-please to trigger CI on its PRs (#64)

## Summary
- PRs created by the default `GITHUB_TOKEN` don't trigger other
workflows (GitHub's infinite loop prevention)
- This is why release-please PRs always show "Waiting for status to be
reported" and CI never runs
- Using the org-wide `RELEASE_PLEASE_TOKEN` PAT so release-please PRs
trigger CI automatically

## Test plan
- [ ] Merge a fix PR to main, verify the release-please PR gets CI
checks triggered automatically

commit.message:
feat(embedding): add Ollama embedding provider and expand docs (#56)

### Motivation
- Add support for Ollama-hosted embeddings to provide a fully
local/self-hosted embedding option alongside OpenAI and FastEmbed.
- Give operators clear guidance on embedding provider tradeoffs,
embedding dimensions, and hosting implications (Railway vs self-hosted).
- Integrate Ollama into the provider selection flow so auto and explicit
modes can use a running Ollama service.

### Description
- Added `automem/embedding/ollama.py` implementing
`OllamaEmbeddingProvider` with request retries, timeout, and response
format handling.
- Exported `OllamaEmbeddingProvider` from
`automem/embedding/__init__.py` and integrated explicit
`EMBEDDING_PROVIDER=ollama` handling in `init_embedding_provider()` in
`app.py`.
- Extended auto-selection in `app.py` to attempt Ollama when
`OLLAMA_BASE_URL` or `OLLAMA_MODEL` is configured, and updated valid
`EMBEDDING_PROVIDER` options.
- Updated docs (`docs/ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLES.md`, `docs/QDRANT_SETUP.md`,
`README.md`) to document new env vars (`OLLAMA_BASE_URL`,
`OLLAMA_MODEL`, `OLLAMA_TIMEOUT`, `OLLAMA_MAX_RETRIES`), embedding
tradeoffs, dimension notes, and hosting considerations.

### Testing
- No automated tests were executed for this change.

commit.message:
feat: Add `exclude_tags` parameter to recall API (#58)

## Summary

Adds support for excluding specific tags in the `/recall` endpoint to
enable efficient cross-conversation memory retrieval without client-side
filtering.

## Motivation

When building multi-conversation chat applications, we need to recall:
- **Short-term memories**: Only from the current conversation
- **Long-term memories**: From ALL conversations EXCEPT the current one

Currently, the recall API only supports positive tag filtering. To
achieve cross-conversation recall while excluding the current
conversation, clients must:
1. Request more results than needed (e.g., 10 instead of 3)
2. Filter out unwanted tags client-side
3. Trim to desired limit

This is inefficient and can miss relevant memories if the current
conversation dominates recent activity.

## Changes

### Core Implementation

#### 1. Parameter Parsing (`automem/api/recall.py`)
- Added `exclude_tags` parameter parsing in `handle_recall()` function
- Supports comma-separated values: `?exclude_tags=conv1,conv2`
- Supports multiple parameters: `?exclude_tags=conv1&exclude_tags=conv2`

#### 2. Filter Logic (`app.py`)
- Enhanced `_result_passes_filters()` to accept and process
`exclude_tags`
- Implements exclusion logic with both exact and prefix matching
- Excludes if ANY excluded tag is found (OR logic)

#### 3. Filter Application (`automem/api/recall.py`)
- Applied exclusion filter to all search paths:
  - Vector search results
  - Graph keyword search results
  - Tag-only search results
  - Relation expansion results
  - Entity expansion results

### Testing

Added comprehensive test coverage in `tests/test_api_endpoints.py`:
- `test_recall_with_exclude_tags_single` - Single tag exclusion
- `test_recall_with_exclude_tags_multiple` - Multiple tag exclusions
- `test_recall_with_exclude_tags_prefix_matching` - Prefix-based
exclusion
- `test_recall_with_tags_and_exclude_tags_combined` - Combined positive
and negative filtering
- `test_recall_exclude_tags_with_no_results` - Edge case handling

**Test Results:**
```
14 passed in 1.58s
✅ All existing recall tests pass
✅ All new exclude_tags tests pass
```

### Documentation

Updated `docs/API.md`:
- Added `exclude_tags` to recall endpoint parameters
- Included usage examples
- Documented behavior and matching rules

## API Changes

### New Parameter

**`exclude_tags`** (optional)
- Type: `string` or `string[]`
- Behavior: Excludes memories containing ANY of the specified tags
- Matching: Both exact and prefix matching supported
- Logic: OR-based (excludes if any tag matches)

### Examples

```bash
# Exclude single conversation
GET /recall?tags=user_1&exclude_tags=conversation_5&limit=3

# Exclude multiple conversations (comma-separated)
GET /recall?tags=user_1&exclude_tags=conversation_5,conversation_6&limit=5

# Exclude assistant messages from a conversation
GET /recall?tags=conversation_3&exclude_tags=assistant&limit=10

# Exclude temporary/draft memories (prefix matching)
GET /recall?query=project%20plan&exclude_tags=temp,draft&limit=10
```

## Behavior

### Exclusion Rules
1. **Exact Match**: Tag exactly matches an excluded tag
2. **Prefix Match**: Tag starts with an excluded tag (e.g., `temp`
excludes `temp:draft`)
3. **OR Logic**: Memory is excluded if ANY excluded tag matches

### Filter Order
1. Time filtering (if specified)
2. Positive tag filtering (if specified)
3. Exclusion filtering (if specified)

## Backward Compatibility

✅ **Fully backward compatible**
- New parameter is optional (defaults to empty list)
- Existing API calls work unchanged
- No changes to response format
- All existing tests pass

## Use Cases

1. **Cross-conversation recall**: Get memories from all conversations
except current
2. **Exclude low-importance**: Filter out temporary, draft, or test
memories
3. **Role-based filtering**: Exclude assistant messages, only get user
messages
4. **Conversation summaries**: Build summaries excluding meta/noise tags
5. **"Forget this conversation"**: Exclude specific conversation from
results

## Performance

- **Minimal overhead**: Simple set/prefix comparison
- **More efficient than client-side filtering**: No over-fetching
- **Accurate results**: Gets correct memories even when excluded items
dominate

## Related Issues

Resolves https://github.com/verygoodplugins/automem/issues/57

- [x] Implementation complete
- [x] All existing tests pass
- [x] New tests added with full coverage
- [x] Documentation updated
- [x] API examples provided
- [x] Backward compatible
- [x] No breaking changes
- [x] Code follows existing patterns
- [x] Type hints maintained

commit.message:
fix(mcp): replace broken res.on('close') with TTL sweep for Streamable HTTP sessions (#59)

## Summary

- **Bug:** The Streamable HTTP endpoint's `res.on('close')` handler was
destroying sessions after every HTTP response. Unlike SSE (long-lived
connection), Streamable HTTP uses standard request/response cycles, so
this handler fired on every response completion — making session reuse
impossible.
- **Fix:** Replaced with a TTL-based session sweep that cleans up idle
sessions after 1 hour, with `lastAccess` tracking on each request. The
`transport.onclose` handler remains for explicit transport close events.

## Changes

- Removed `res.on('close')` session cleanup for Streamable HTTP
transport
- Added `lastAccess` timestamp to session tracking (set on init and each
request)
- Added background sweep interval (every 5 min) cleaning sessions idle
>1 hour
- `unref()` on timer to avoid blocking Node.js process exit

## Test plan

- [x] Verify Streamable HTTP sessions persist across multiple requests
(session ID reuse works)
- [x] Verify sessions are cleaned up after 1 hour of inactivity
- [x] Verify SSE sessions still clean up correctly via `res.on('close')`
(unchanged)
- [x] Verify `transport.onclose` still fires for explicit transport
close events

commit.message:
fix(mcp): use Promise.resolve().catch() for async close() in session sweep

Sync try/catch doesn't catch promise rejections from transport.close()
and server.close() which return Promise<void>.

fix(mcp): close transport/server on sweep and update Accept header test

- Session sweep now calls transport.close() and server.close() before
  deleting the entry and cleaning the event store, preventing resource
  leaks. Each call is guarded with existence checks and try/catch.
  sessions.delete() stays first so transport.onclose's sessions.has()
  guard prevents double-cleanup.
- Update Accept header test expectation from 400 to 406 to match
  current MCP SDK behavior (406 Not Acceptable).

Co-authored-by: Cursor <cursoragent@cursor.com>

fix(mcp): replace broken res.on('close') with TTL sweep for Streamable HTTP sessions

The Streamable HTTP transport uses standard request/response cycles, not
long-lived connections like SSE. The res.on('close') handler was firing
at the end of every HTTP response, immediately destroying the session
and making session reuse impossible.

Replace with:
- lastAccess timestamp tracking on session init and each request
- Background sweep (every 5 min) that cleans up sessions idle >1 hour
- unref() on the timer to avoid blocking process exit

The transport.onclose handler remains for explicit close events.

Co-authored-by: Cursor <cursoragent@cursor.com>

fix: Production bugs - datetime tz, OpenAI tokens, Qdrant race

Three critical bugs found in Railway production logs:

1. **DateTime TypeError (500s)**
   - `_parse_iso_datetime()` now assumes UTC for naive timestamps
   - Fixes: "can't compare offset-naive and offset-aware datetimes"

2. **OpenAI max_tokens 400 error**
   - Use `max_completion_tokens` for o-series/gpt-5 models
   - Use `max_tokens` for older models (gpt-4o-mini, etc.)

3. **Qdrant 404 race condition**
   - Handle UnexpectedResponse 404 gracefully (point not yet uploaded)
   - Log as debug instead of error for expected race condition

Added test coverage for naive datetime handling.

commit.message:
fix: Refine summarization token limit and improve tests

Updated summarize_content to estimate max_tokens based on target character length, capping at 150 tokens. Test cases now use authorization headers and properly patch openai_client to ensure summarization logic is exercised. Also switched to using the app instance directly in tests for consistency.

feat: add memory content size governance with auto-summarization

Implements backend enforcement of memory content size limits:

## Config (automem/config.py)
- MEMORY_CONTENT_SOFT_LIMIT: 500 chars (triggers auto-summarization)
- MEMORY_CONTENT_HARD_LIMIT: 2000 chars (rejects with 400)
- MEMORY_AUTO_SUMMARIZE: true/false toggle
- MEMORY_SUMMARY_TARGET_LENGTH: 300 chars target

## Summarization (automem/utils/text.py)
- summarize_content(): Uses LLM to condense oversized content
- should_summarize_content(): Checks content against limits

## Store Endpoint (automem/api/memory.py)
- Rejects content exceeding hard limit with descriptive 400 error
- Auto-summarizes content above soft limit (if enabled)
- Preserves original_content in metadata for audit trail
- Response includes summarized=true and length info when summarized

## Tests (tests/test_content_size.py)
- Unit tests for should_summarize_content helper
- Unit tests for summarize_content function
- Integration test fixtures (skipped in CI without fixtures)

Why: Large memories cause semantic dilution, context bloat, and
information loss on retrieval. This ensures high-quality, focused
memories with automatic condensation of oversized content.

readme_html:
AI Memory That Actually Learns

AutoMem is a production-grade long-term memory system for AI assistants with transparent LoCoMo benchmark baselines (ACL 2024): 89.27% on locomo-mini categories 1-4 with category 5 skipped, and 87.56% on full locomo with the opt-in category-5 judge enabled. See benchmarks/EXPERIMENT_LOG.md for methodology and current baselines.

Deploy in 60 seconds:

railway up

Graph Viewer (Standalone)

The visualizer now runs as a separate service/repository (automem-graph-viewer). AutoMem keeps /viewer as a compatibility entrypoint and forwards users to the standalone app.

Set these variables on the AutoMem API service:

ENABLE_GRAPH_VIEWER=true
GRAPH_VIEWER_URL=https://<your-viewer-domain>
VIEWER_ALLOWED_ORIGINS=https://<your-viewer-domain>

When users open /viewer/#token=..., AutoMem preserves the hash token and redirects to the standalone viewer with server=<automem-origin>.

Why AutoMem Exists

Your AI forgets everything between sessions. RAG dumps similar documents. Vector databases match keywords but miss meaning. None of them learn.

AutoMem gives AI assistants the ability to remember, connect, and evolve their understanding over time—just like human long-term memory.

How AutoMem Works

AutoMem combines two complementary storage systems:

  • FalkorDB (Graph) - Stores memories as nodes with typed relationships between them
  • Qdrant (Vectors) - Enables semantic similarity search via embeddings

This dual architecture lets you ask questions like "why did we choose PostgreSQL?" and get not just the memory, but the reasoning, preferences, and related decisions that informed it.

Core Capabilities

  • 🧠 Store memories with metadata, importance scores, and temporal context
  • 🔍 Recall via hybrid search combining semantic, keyword, graph, and temporal signals
  • 🔗 Connect memories with 11 authorable relationship types, plus system-generated semantic and temporal edges
  • 🎯 Learn through automatic entity extraction, pattern detection, and consolidation
  • ⚡ Perform with sub-100ms recall across thousands of memories

Research Foundation

AutoMem implements techniques from peer-reviewed memory research:

  • HippoRAG 2 (Ohio State, 2025): Graph-vector hybrid for associative memory
  • A-MEM (2025): Dynamic memory organization with Zettelkasten-inspired clustering
  • MELODI (DeepMind, 2024): Compression via gist representations
  • ReadAgent (DeepMind, 2024): Context extension through episodic memory

Architecture

flowchart TB
    subgraph service [AutoMem Service Flask]
        API[REST API<br/>Memory Lifecycle]
        Enrichment[Background Enrichment<br/>Pipeline]
        Consolidation[Consolidation<br/>Engine]
        Backups[Automated Backups<br/>Optional]
    end

    subgraph storage [Dual Storage Layer]
        FalkorDB[(FalkorDB<br/>Graph Database)]
        Qdrant[(Qdrant<br/>Vector Database)]
    end

    Client[AI Client] -->|Store/Recall/Associate| API
    API --> FalkorDB
    API --> Qdrant
    Enrichment -->|11 edge types<br/>Pattern nodes| FalkorDB
    Enrichment -->|Semantic search<br/>3072-d vectors| Qdrant
    Consolidation --> FalkorDB
    Consolidation --> Qdrant
    Backups -.->|Optional| FalkorDB
    Backups -.->|Optional| Qdrant

FalkorDB (graph) = canonical record, relationships, consolidation Qdrant (vectors) = semantic recall, similarity search Dual storage = Built-in redundancy and disaster recovery

Why Graph + Vector?

        VectorDB1[(Vector DB)]
        Result1[✅ PostgreSQL memory<br/>❌ No reasoning<br/>❌ No connections]

        Query1 -->|Similarity search| VectorDB1
        VectorDB1 --> Result1
    end

    subgraph automem [AutoMem Graph + Vector]
        direction TB

        subgraph hybrid [Hybrid Search]
            VectorDB2[(Qdrant<br/>Vectors)]
            GraphDB2[(FalkorDB<br/>Graph)]
        end

        Result2[✅ PostgreSQL memory<br/>✅ PREFERS_OVER MongoDB<br/>✅ RELATES_TO team expertise<br/>✅ DERIVED_FROM boring tech]

Traditional RAG (Vector Only)

Memory: "Chose PostgreSQL for reliability"
Query: "What database should I use?"
Result: ✅ Finds the memory
         ❌ Doesn't know WHY you chose it
         ❌ Can't connect to related decisions

AutoMem (Graph + Vector)

Memory: "Chose PostgreSQL for reliability"
Graph: PREFERS_OVER MongoDB
       RELATES_TO "team expertise" memory
       DERIVED_FROM "boring technology" principle

Query: "What database should I use?"
Result: ✅ Finds the memory
        ✅ Knows your decision factors
        ✅ Shows related preferences
        ✅ Explains your reasoning pattern

How It Works in Practice

Multi-Hop Bridge Discovery

When you ask a question, AutoMem doesn't just find relevant memories—it finds the connections between them. This is called bridge discovery: following graph relationships to surface memories that link your initial results together.

graph TB
    Query[User Query:<br/>Why boring tech for Kafka?]

    Seed1[Seed Memory 1:<br/>PostgreSQL migration<br/>for operational simplicity]

    Seed2[Seed Memory 2:<br/>Kafka vs RabbitMQ<br/>evaluation]

    Bridge[Bridge Memory:<br/>Team prefers boring technology<br/>proven, debuggable systems]

    Result[Result:<br/>AI understands architectural<br/>philosophy, not just isolated choices]

    Query -->|Initial recall| Seed1
    Query -->|Initial recall| Seed2
    Seed1 -.->|DERIVED_FROM| Bridge
    Seed2 -.->|DERIVED_FROM| Bridge
    Bridge --> Result
    Seed1 --> Result
    Seed2 --> Result

Traditional RAG: Returns "Kafka" memories (misses the connection)

AutoMem bridge discovery:

  • Seed 1: "Migrated to PostgreSQL for operational simplicity"
  • Seed 2: "Evaluating Kafka vs RabbitMQ for message queue"
  • Bridge: "Team prefers boring technology—proven, debuggable systems"

AutoMem finds the bridge that connects both decisions → Result: AI understands your architectural philosophy, not just isolated choices

How to enable:

  • Set expand_relations=true in recall requests (enabled by default)
  • Control depth with relation_limit and expansion_limit parameters
  • Results are ranked by relation strength, temporal relevance, and importance

Knowledge Graphs That Evolve

# After storing: "Migrated to PostgreSQL for operational simplicity"

AutoMem automatically:
├── Extracts entities (PostgreSQL, operational simplicity)
├── Auto-tags (entity:tool:postgresql, entity:concept:ops-simplicity)
├── Detects pattern ("prefers boring technology")
├── Links temporally (PRECEDED_BY migration planning)
└── Connects semantically (SIMILAR_TO "Redis deployment")

# Next query: "Should we use Kafka?"
AI recalls:
- PostgreSQL decision
- "Boring tech" pattern (reinforced across memories)
- Operational simplicity preference
→ Suggests: "Based on your pattern, consider RabbitMQ instead"

9-Component Hybrid Scoring

    end

GET /recall?query=database%20migration&tags=decision&time_query=last%20month

# AutoMem combines nine signals:
score = vector×0.25       # Semantic similarity
      + keyword×0.15      # TF-IDF text matching
      + relation×0.25     # Graph relationship strength
      + content×0.25      # Direct token overlap
      + temporal×0.15     # Time alignment with query
      + tag×0.10          # Tag matching
      + importance×0.05   # User-assigned priority
      + confidence×0.05   # Memory confidence
      + recency×0.10      # Freshness boost

# Result: Memories ranked by meaning, not just similarity

Features

Core Memory Operations

  • Store - Rich memories with metadata, importance, timestamps, embeddings
  • Recall - Hybrid search (vector + keyword + tags + time windows)
  • Update - Modify memories, auto-regenerate embeddings
  • Delete - Remove from both graph and vector stores
  • Associate - Create typed relationships between memories
  • Filter - Tag-based queries with prefix/exact matching

Memory Consolidation

AutoMem uses neuroscience-inspired consolidation cycles—like human sleep—to keep memories relevant:

Cycle Frequency Purpose
Decay Daily Exponential relevance scoring (age, access, connections, importance)
Creative Weekly REM-like processing that discovers non-obvious connections
Cluster Monthly Groups similar memories, generates meta-patterns
Forget Disabled Archives low-relevance (<0.2), deletes very old (<0.05) when enabled

How it works:

  • Wrong rabbit holes fade naturally (~30-45 days without access)
  • Important memories with strong connections stay indefinitely
  • Memories archive before deletion (0.05-0.2 relevance range)
  • The system learns what matters to you, not what you explicitly tag

Background Intelligence

Every memory gets automatically enhanced in the background (doesn't block your API calls):

Enrichment Pipeline (runs immediately after storage):

  • Entity extraction - Identifies people, projects, tools, concepts (spaCy NLP)
  • Auto-tagging - Generates entity:<type>:<slug> for structured queries
  • Summaries - Lightweight gist representations for quick scanning
  • Temporal links - Connects to recent memories with PRECEDED_BY relationships
  • Semantic neighbors - Finds similar memories via cosine similarity (SIMILAR_TO)
  • Pattern detection - Reinforces emerging themes across your memory graph

Consolidation Engine (runs on configurable schedules):

  • See Memory Consolidation section above

11 Authorable Relationship Types

Build rich knowledge graphs:

Type Use Case Example
RELATES_TO General connection Bug report → Related issue
LEADS_TO Causal relationship Problem → Solution
OCCURRED_BEFORE Temporal sequence Planning → Execution
PREFERS_OVER User preferences PostgreSQL → MongoDB
EXEMPLIFIES Pattern examples Code review → Best practice
CONTRADICTS Conflicting info Old approach → New approach
REINFORCES Supporting evidence Decision → Validation
INVALIDATED_BY Outdated info Legacy docs → Current docs
EVOLVED_INTO Knowledge evolution Initial design → Final design
DERIVED_FROM Source tracking Implementation → Spec
PART_OF Hierarchical structure Feature → Epic

System-generated relationship types:

  • SIMILAR_TO - Semantic neighbors created by enrichment
  • PRECEDED_BY - Temporal links created by enrichment
  • DISCOVERED - Internal heuristic edges created by consolidation (kind=explains|shares_theme|parallel_context)

Quick Start

Option 1: Railway (Recommended)

Deploy AutoMem to Railway in 60 seconds:

This deploys 3 services automatically:

  • memory-service — Core AutoMem API
  • falkordb — Graph database with persistent storage
  • mcp-sse-server — MCP bridge for ChatGPT, Claude.ai, ElevenLabs

👉 Deployment Guide for detailed Railway setup

Option 2: Docker Compose (Local)

Run everything locally:

# Clone and start services
git clone https://github.com/verygoodplugins/automem.git
cd automem
make dev

# API: http://localhost:8001
# FalkorDB: localhost:6379
# Qdrant: localhost:6333

Option 3: Development Mode

Run API without Docker:

python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements-dev.txt
PORT=8001 python app.py

API Examples

Store a Memory

curl -X POST http://localhost:8001/memory \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "content": "Chose PostgreSQL over MongoDB for ACID compliance",
    "type": "Decision",
    "confidence": 0.95,
    "tags": ["database", "architecture"],
    "importance": 0.9,
    "metadata": {
      "source": "architecture-meeting",
      "alternatives": ["MongoDB", "MySQL"],
      "deciding_factors": ["ACID", "team_expertise"]
    }
  }'

Available memory types: Decision, Pattern, Preference, Style, Habit, Insight, Context (default)

  • Explicit type recommended when you know the classification
  • Omit type to let enrichment auto-classify from content

Recall Memories

# Hybrid search with tags and time
GET /recall?query=database&tags=decision&time_query=last%20month

# Semantic search with vector
GET /recall?embedding=0.12,0.56,...&limit=10

# Tag prefix matching (finds slack:U123:*, slack:channel-ops, etc.)
GET /recall?tags=slack&tag_match=prefix

# Graph expansion with filtering (reduce noise in related memories)
GET /recall?query=auth%20architecture&expand_relations=true&expand_min_importance=0.5&expand_min_strength=0.3

# Multi-hop entity expansion (e.g., "What is Sarah's sister's job?")
GET /recall?query=What%20is%20Sarah%27s%20sister%27s%20job&expand_entities=true

Create Relationship

curl -X POST http://localhost:8001/associate \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "memory1_id": "uuid-postgres-decision",
    "memory2_id": "uuid-mongodb-evaluation",
    "type": "PREFERS_OVER",
    "strength": 0.9
  }'

Use With AI Platforms

AutoMem works with any AI platform via:

MCP (Model Context Protocol)

Local MCP Bridge (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Claude Code):

# Install official MCP bridge
npm install -g @verygoodplugins/mcp-automem

# Configure for local AI tools
npx @verygoodplugins/mcp-automem setup

Remote MCP (Cloud AI Platforms):

Connect AutoMem to cloud AI platforms via HTTPS. Works with:

  • ChatGPT (requires developer mode)
  • Claude.ai web interface
  • Claude mobile app
  • ElevenLabs Agents

See Remote MCP documentation for setup instructions.

👉 Resources:

  • NPM bridge (local): @verygoodplugins/mcp-automem
  • Remote MCP setup: docs/MCP_SSE.md

Direct API

Any language, any framework:

import requests

response = requests.post(
    "https://your-automem.railway.app/memory",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"},
    json={"content": "Memory content", "importance": 0.8}
)

Comparison with Alternatives

vs. Traditional RAG Systems

Traditional RAG retrieves similar documents. AutoMem understands relationships:

RAG: "Here are 5 documents about PostgreSQL" AutoMem: "You chose PostgreSQL over MongoDB because you prefer boring technology for operational simplicity. This pattern also influenced your Redis and RabbitMQ decisions."

  • ✅ Typed relationships - Not just "similar", but "causes", "contradicts", "evolved from"
  • ✅ Temporal awareness - Knows what preceded, what invalidated, what emerged
  • ✅ Pattern learning - Discovers your preferences and decision-making style
  • ✅ Consolidation - Memories strengthen or fade based on use—like human memory

vs. Vector Databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant)

Vector databases match embeddings. AutoMem builds knowledge graphs:

  • ✅ Multi-hop reasoning - Bridge discovery connects memories across conversation threads
  • ✅ 11 relationship types - Structured semantics vs. cosine similarity alone

  • ⚡ Sub-100ms recall - Even with 100k+ memories

Research Background

AutoMem's architecture is based on peer-reviewed research in memory systems and graph theory:

HippoRAG 2 (Ohio State, June 2025)

Finding: Graph-vector hybrid achieves 7% better associative memory than pure vector RAG, approaching human long-term memory performance.

AutoMem implementation: Dual FalkorDB (graph) + Qdrant (vector) architecture with 11 typed relationship edges.

A-MEM (July 2025)

Finding: Dynamic memory organization with Zettelkasten principles enables emergent knowledge structures.

AutoMem implementation: Pattern detection, clustering cycles, and automatic entity linking that builds knowledge graphs from conversation.

MELODI (DeepMind, 2024)

Finding: 8x memory compression without quality loss through gist representations and selective preservation.

AutoMem implementation: Summary generation, importance scoring, and consolidation cycles that strengthen relevant memories while fading noise.

ReadAgent (DeepMind, 2024)

Finding: 20x context extension via episodic memory and temporal organization.

AutoMem implementation: Temporal relationship types (PRECEDED_BY, OCCURRED_BEFORE) and time-aware scoring that preserves conversation flow.
